Ep.158 https://youtu.be/u3xeXtQ4ue8\n\nWe started living off the grid here in the Canadian wilderness four years ago and in the first year we couldn't grow a garden because the conifer trees were too tall and shaded everything out! Then, last year we filled the raised beds (we milled with our chainsaw) with too many wood chips making the carbon levels too high in the soil. Now the wood chips have composted and our Hügelkultur principals are giving us an abundant garden harvest! Nicolle lead you on a tour of the garden and harvest at the end of the video! Jake also freedives and spearfishes and puts away the most firewood so far!!! Fox is making us laugh all day! Canadian wilderness four years ago and in the first year we couldn't grow a garden because the conifer trees were too tall and shaded everything out! Then, last year we filled the raised beds (we milled with our chainsaw) with too many wood chips making the carbon levels too high in the soil. Now the wood chips have composted and our Hügelkultur principals are giving us an abundant garden harvest! Nicolle lead you on a tour of the garden and harvest at the end of the video! - Wie JEDER (trotz Austritt) die Kirche finanziert: Kind: captions Language: en Did you know that public church institutions such as kindergartens or hospitals are allowed to fire employees if they have had an abortion or are simply getting a divorce? And did you know that these institutions are not financed by church tax, but by public funds? From every citizen in Germany, regardless of religion? If not, then get ready for an exciting video. First of all, I would like to say that I don't care at all whether or not you belong to any religion, and if so, which one . This video has absolutely nothing to do with belief in oneself. Furthermore, I do not want to deny that some professional groups within the church, such as pastors, priests, or employees in church hospitals, etc., do not do super important work. This video is simply about the institutional church and how it is intertwined with the state in Germany, and what absurd rights the church sometimes has. Rights that are actually incompatible with normal labor law. But let's start from the beginning. I just stated that everyone in Germany pays the church. And that's something many people don't even know. According to the BPB, a total of 543,000 people left the church in 2019. And the trend of high departure numbers continues, not least due to various missteps that the church has made in recent years. I mean, first there were the various cases of abuse that were uncovered, but then some of them still were n't properly dealt with. And then there are always those knee-slappers, like the Archdiocese of Cologne slapping down a priest's gambling debts, which, with taxes and interest, amounted to a total of 1.15 million euros. Yes, that will surely make some YouTubers' eyes widen. It can be assumed that in the not too long term less than 50% of the population in Germany will be church members, because the church tax amounts to 8 or 9% of the wage or income tax payable, depending on the federal state. And I've calculated it for you. This amounts to approximately 12 billion euros in church tax annually. In everyday speech, when people leave a club, you often hear phrases like, " Well, I no longer want to financially support this club." Well, Herbert, I'm afraid I have to disappoint you, because as long as you work and pay taxes, even if you're not a church member, you pay a portion of your income to the church. Because very few so-called church dignitaries such as bishops or cardinals know that they are not paid by the church and its church tax, but almost always by the individual federal states or by the state. The federal states naturally get this money from every citizen who pays taxes, and these amounts add up . So, let me put it this way, the monthly gross salary of a bishop starts at around €8000. Cardinals earn up to €15,000 per month. Yes, then you can certainly afford a golden bathtub. In 2020, around 570 million euros in state payments flowed from the federal states to the church, i.e., over half a billion euros apart from church tax. And this absurd payment arrangement dates back, among other things, to an agreement between the church and the state that is over 200 years old. I will now explain it briefly and simply , because it is complex. After two coalition wars against France and Napoleon, Austria and Prussia lost left-bank Rhine territories, which at that time were still principalities or belonged to the church, to France. This led to the Final Recess of the Imperial Deputation in 1803 and the beginning of secularization. This means that more and more church territories and property were confiscated and nationalized by the state, and the church was compensated monetarily with regular payments. These regular payments were actually supposed to be abolished with the Weimar Constitution in 1919 and compensated by a one-off payment . But that has n't happened to this day. Yeah, 100 years is a good age to go , right? Civil servants at work. No, just kidding. Of course, it's more complicated, because a one-off payment would probably be about 20 times higher than the current annual payments. So, a one-time payment of 10 billion instead of half a billion annually. And also, I've done the math, even if it would pay off in 20 years, nobody wants to do it now. That 's a typical phenomenon in politics. Anything that's expensive right now should be postponed; the next government can do it. Climate change. Hm, but that's not all. We now have the church tax, which only church members pay, and the half a billion in state subsidies, which virtually everyone pays. But church institutions such as kindergartens, schools, senior citizens' homes and hospitals are financed differently. These institutions, like all other public institutions with external sponsors, are subsidized by the state. In Germany, to put it simply, the state neither wants to nor can manage all public institutions itself . That means he delegates tasks. For example, in addition to municipal schools, there are also private and church-run schools. In addition to municipal hospitals, there are also private and church-run hospitals. And that's a good thing, at least initially. Then, as a private individual, you can decide where you would most like to go. Sometimes, of course, you do n't have a wide selection due to location. Because such public institutions are actually the responsibility of the state, private or church-run organizations are subsidized by the state . So much for, oh, you're taking on state responsibilities for the common good. We think that's great, then we don't have so much work and in return we pay you a lot of what you need. And now for the kicker . Thus, the costs of church-run daycare centers are 90 to 100% covered by the state, the costs of church-run schools are more than 98% covered by the state, and the costs of church-run hospitals are 100% covered by Start and health insurance companies. And that 's a lot of money overall, because there are a great many public church institutions in Germany. Approximately every second daycare center and roughly every fourth hospital is run by a church organization. These organizations are usually Caritas for the Catholic Church or Diakonie for the Protestant Church. The figures are hard to find, but roughly speaking, between 45 and 65 billion euros in direct and indirect subsidies go to Caritas Diakonie, its institutions and its employees every year. So, approximately 50 billion euros, not from church tax, but from the tax revenue of all citizens. And actually, that would even be okay if all citizens who pay for it could use these church facilities. Ha-Ha. But is that so? Because, let me put it this way, in church institutions, the Basic Law often doesn't apply, even though the state finances almost everything; instead, the law of the church prevails. That's the Kcks and now we're going to do a little storytime. I'll tell you the story of kindergarten director Bernadette K. Since the kindergarten where she worked was Catholic, she had to sign a contract stating that she would live her life according to Catholic rules and exemplify them for the children . However, in 2011 Bernadette separated from her husband and found a new partner. As a result, the termination notice promptly arrives. Objective elements of adultery. The reasoning is: Anyone who ends their Catholic marriage can no longer be a good role model and is not entitled to run a kindergarten. Nice. In a letter to the responsible diocese in Cologne, this issue was formulated as follows . Dismissal of Bernadette K. due to the risk of causing harmful annoyance in the community. I've reflected on my life and considered, am I really harmful? followed me around for a very long time. Being a harmful nuisance will stay with me for the rest of my life. And it gets even more absurd, although the church terminates Bernadette's contract, they are mercifully trying to find Bernadette a new home in another city, in another Catholic kindergarten . The reporter in the documentary about this case then asks whether that isn't totally absurd. And the responsible driver then replies something like: "Yes, the divorce is bad, but as long as no one in the new city has found out about it , it doesn't matter, as long as we can assume that people at work also don't know anything about these living conditions, then the danger of this annoyance is not present." So the cover-up is more important than the problem itself. Hmm. We've seen that before in relation to the church, haven't we ? Well, the absurd labor law of the church goes so far that in this Catholic primary school, a Protestant substitute teacher cannot be hired despite the need for teachers , simply because she has the wrong denomination. He thinks highly of her work and would have liked to hire her permanently , but for non-Helfa applicants, even an interview is not possible. Yes, my first thought was to convert, to become Catholic, but that can't be the reason . I know that many colleagues do that. You wouldn't really think that's still the case nowadays . And just imagine, in Germany over a million people work in such public institutions run by churches. I don't mean clergy like drivers or anything, but just ordinary employees in these facilities, in retirement homes, hospitals, etc. Over a million people who have to fear not being hired or being fired if they renounce or change their religion. Thousands of people who could be fired because of their homosexuality. Thousands of women who could lose their jobs after having an abortion. Or, as Pastor Schiffers puts it, only people who share our most important values are allowed to work in our institutions. Even if these people are paid by the state Yes, of couse. Then the state would have to consider whether we would do that , right? And now, to reiterate, if it were a private club with such strange working conditions, but all members agreed to them , then it wouldn't matter at all. Let them do what they want. But these workers are financed almost exclusively by the state, that is, by all of us , yet the church is allowed to make the rules. I believe that if we citizens are all financing this, then the Basic Law and general labor law should also apply. Then no one should be allowed to discriminate against anyone based on their religious affiliation, nor on their sexuality or on things that happen in their private life. How absurd is that in 2022? I almost lost my faith when I read that. In case anyone is wondering what the legal basis for this is, it is literally the case that the church in Germany has a separate labor law , or rather, exceptions to labor law apply. Thus, Article 140 of our Basic Law contains an incorporated article of the Wahlmar Reich Constitution. And it states, among other things: "Each religious community organizes and manages its affairs independently within the limits of the law applicable to all . It confers its offices without the involvement of the state or the civil community." So, as a religious community, the church does not necessarily have to adhere to normal labor law, but it is financed by the general public. In my opinion, that's insane. But slowly, things are starting to pick up a bit . In 2009, the chief physician of a Catholic hospital was dismissed because he got divorced and remarried . Sorry, I have to laugh because that's totally absurd in itself. Well , he didn't take it lying down and went to court, and the whole thing dragged on for a proud 10 years in court, all the way to the European Court of Justice. First of all, the doctor had been vindicated by the Düsseldorf Labor and Regional Court as well as by the Federal Labor Court. The church then appealed to the Federal Constitutional Court . The church was successful there, because, as we recall, the church has a separate labor law in Germany. However, the federal employee was not satisfied with these requirements from Karlsruhe and appealed to the European Court of Justice. And they then confirmed, in a sense, that this termination was not lawful . Let me put it very simply. The reasoning was something like this: a patient expects medical skills from a chief physician, and it doesn't matter for the profession whether he adheres to the sacrament of marriage. And so, after 10 years, the doctor was vindicated by the federal worker in 2019. This is an important ruling and means that church institutions can no longer simply do whatever they want. Nevertheless, it is of course only a drop in the ocean. How this looks in other individual cases, or for those who don't dare to go to court, or generally regarding the hiring criteria, is a completely different matter .
